hypothesis: PHIL-5a rewritten criterion (S537) escapes Campbell law. DECAYED+BLIND growing faster than MUST+ACTIVE should show convergence trend.
method: Analyzed 59 knowledge_state snapshots from S377-S537. Computed accessibility ratio (MUST+ACTIVE)/(DECAYED+BLIND) per session. Identified phase transitions. Tested 20-session threshold against historical data.

results.note: The S452-S472 window (20 sessions) had ratio<1.0 continuously. The S537 criterion says DROP if ratio<1.0 for 20 sessions. If criterion had existed then, PHIL-5a would have been DROPPED.
results.campbell_law_evidence: Criterion threshold (20 sessions) ≈ historical window duration (20 sessions). Criterion designed AFTER recovery, calibrated to barely NOT trigger on known data.

actual: PHIL-5a criterion was RETROACTIVELY MET in S452-S472 (20 sessions with ratio<1.0). Criterion designed S537, AFTER recovery to 1.48x. Campbell Law: criterion calibrated to just barely not trigger on historical data. Current trend: stable/improving, no imminent DROP risk.
diff: Expected DECAYED growing faster than ACTIVE. Found: HISTORICALLY this happened (S377-S457), but system recovered via structural intervention (compaction + awareness). Current trajectory is stable. The deeper finding: criterion design avoids known failure windows — Campbell Law at criteria level.

verdict.phil5a_criterion_quality: IMPROVED but suspect — measurable, yet retroactively calibrated
verdict.confirmation_attractor_evidence: STRONG — even redesigned criteria are shaped by knowledge of past data
verdict.prediction_0_of_3: Still holding — confirmation attractor remains structural
verdict.novel_contribution: First demonstration that F-EPIS3 criteria rewriting is subject to Campbell Law — the criterion-rewrite loop itself is a confirmation mechanism
